name: A1 Interchange East
client: Republic of Cyprus, Department of Public Works
summary: A three-level grade-separated interchange untangling the capital's eastern approach, delivered across 30 months without a single full closure of the A1.

content.text: The A1 Interchange East reorganises the junction where the island's principal motorway meets Nicosia's eastern ring road. The works comprised 1.9 kilometres of new carriageway, two post-tensioned concrete overpasses, four slip roads and the complete reconstruction of the drainage, ducting and lighting networks beneath them.

The engineering challenge was less the structures than the traffic running through them: 46,000 vehicles a day could not stop. Characom's staged programme kept two lanes live in each direction for the entire build, shifting the running alignment eleven times and casting the main overpass decks in overnight possessions agreed lane by lane with the police and the Department of Public Works.

Both bridges were assembled from match-cast segments manufactured at the group's Nicosia precast yard, cutting four months from the site programme and keeping falsework off the live carriageway. Durability drove the specification throughout, sulphate-resistant concrete, stainless reinforcement in the splash zones and a 120-year design life certified by independent checkers.

Since opening, peak crossing times through the junction have fallen from fourteen minutes to under four, and collisions at the old signalised crossing have dropped to near zero. The interchange was handed over two weeks early and within the contract sum, the way public works should end, and too rarely do.

coverImageAlt: Aerial view of intersecting roads and city blocks at dusk

name: Nicosia Civic Archive
client: Republic of Cyprus, Deputy Ministry of Culture
summary: A 12,400 sq.m home for the state's paper memory: climate-stable vaults below, a public reading room flooded with filtered northern light above.

content.text: The Civic Archive consolidates four scattered state depositories into a single purpose-built repository on the edge of Nicosia's old town: 12,400 square metres of climate-stabilised vaults, conservation workshops and, for the first time in the archive's history, a public reading room open to any citizen with a question about their own past.

Paper is a demanding tenant. The vaults hold 18°C and 50% relative humidity through a Nicosia summer using thermal mass first and machinery second: 600 mm concrete walls, a deep-plan layout that buries the collection at the building's core, and a displacement ventilation system that idles for most of the year. The archive's energy use per square metre is a third of the facilities it replaces.

Where the public meets the building, the concrete was treated as a finish, not a structure to be hidden, board-marked with timber from a single Troodos sawmill, its grain running in the direction of each room's light. Bronze solar screens on the southern face will streak and soften over decades, a facade designed to age in public.

The archive opened in autumn 2024 with an exhibition of village land registries dating to the 1880s. School visits are booked out months ahead, the outcome the Deputy Ministry wanted most, and the one no specification clause could guarantee.

name: Larnaca Coastal Defence Works
client: Republic of Cyprus, Larnaca District Administration
summary: 3.2 kilometres of groynes, revetments and beach nourishment defending Larnaca's eastern shore, with a rebuilt public promenade riding above the armour stone.

content.text: Larnaca's eastern shoreline has retreated by up to nine metres in two decades. The Coastal Defence Works answer with a 3.2-kilometre system of submerged groynes, rock revetments and phased beach nourishment, capped by a rebuilt promenade that returns the seafront to pedestrians rather than surrendering it to the sea wall.

The marine method was the tender's deciding card. The heaviest armour stone, individual blocks of up to eight tonnes, is placed from a jack-up barge working seaward of the swimmers' season, guided by GPS-positioned grabs against a wave-modelled design profile. Placing from the water halves truck movements through the tourist district and keeps the beach open through both summers of the programme.

All stone is sourced from licensed Cypriot quarries and every load is photographed, weighed and logged against the environmental permit. Marine ecologists survey the adjacent posidonia meadows quarterly; the design deliberately steers the groyne field away from them, trading a longer structure for an intact seabed.

The first kilometre of nourished beach opens next spring. When the works complete in 2027, the district administration will hold something rare in coastal engineering: a defence line that reads, from the promenade, as simply more beach.

coverImageAlt: Aerial view of a sandy coastline meeting turquoise water

name: Amara Bay Residences
summary: Two stepped residential towers over a landscaped seafront podium, 148 apartments arranged so that every living room watches the water.

content.text: Amara Bay sets two residential towers of fourteen and eleven storeys on a shared podium along Limassol's western seafront: 148 apartments, a residents' club, a 25-metre pool and 4,000 square metres of public colonnade and gardens at street level. The towers step back as they rise, so that the tallest floors read from the beach as terraces rather than wall.

The plan is organised around a single rule set early with the architects: no primary living space faces away from the sea. Achieving it in a double-tower scheme demanded a switchback core layout and transfer structures at levels five and eight, post-tensioned slabs spanning 12.5 metres, poured in summer nights to control thermal cracking, then surveyed at dawn before the sun could argue with the instruments.

Salt is the quiet enemy of seafront building. The envelope answers with marine-grade anodised aluminium, ventilated stone rainscreens and glazing certified against wind-driven spray, while podium planting uses native species that drink brackish irrigation without complaint. The specification adds cost no buyer sees on day one, and value every owner keeps at year thirty.

The development sold out eight months before handover. More telling: the ground-floor colonnade, open to the public by design, has become the neighbourhood's default evening walk, a private project paying a public dividend.

name: Aegean Gate Business Centre
summary: A 21,000 sq.m Grade-A office campus on Nicosia's ring road, two glass volumes around a planted courtyard, built to hold a 40°C summer at ease.

content.text: Aegean Gate brings Grade-A office space to the capital's western ring road: two six-storey volumes joined by a planted courtyard, 21,000 square metres of lettable space, and parking decked below a landscaped roof rather than sprawled around the site. It was, at completion, the largest speculative office development in Nicosia in a decade.

Glass towers and Nicosia summers are natural enemies, so the facade earns its keep: high-performance double-skin glazing on the south and west faces with motorised blinds in the cavity, and a courtyard section shaped to pull a through-breeze across the entrance level. The building's measured cooling load runs 35% below the local benchmark for its class.

Characom acted as both developer's contractor and construction manager, packaging the trades directly and passing tender savings back into the envelope specification. Structure topped out in fourteen months; the first anchor tenant, a regional bank's headquarters, signed before the hoardings came down.

Five years on, the campus holds full occupancy and its courtyard cafe has a lunchtime queue from the neighbouring plots, the surest sign a speculative building has become a fixed point in its district.

name: Paphos Harbour Quarter
summary: A mixed-use waterfront quarter beside Paphos's old fishing harbour, residences, boutique hotel and a public promenade stitched into a protected archaeological setting.

content.text: The Harbour Quarter redevelops six hectares beside Paphos's old fishing harbour into a walkable waterfront district: 212 residences in stepped terraces, a 90-key boutique hotel, ground-floor workshops reserved at protected rents for local makers, and an 800-metre public promenade linking the harbour to the town's archaeological park.

Building beside a UNESCO-listed landscape sets the rules before the architects do. Every foundation position was cleared through supervised trial trenches; when mosaicist workshops surfaced at the site's northern corner, the masterplan was redrawn around them within six weeks and the find is being conserved as the centrepiece of the promenade. Piling switched to pressed-in methods within 200 metres of the ancient harbour wall to keep vibration below archaeological thresholds.

The material palette is deliberately narrow and deliberately local: coursed Paphian limestone, lime-washed render and untreated hardwood shutters, a modern district built from the same shelf of materials as the town behind it. Structural frames are concrete with 30% recycled aggregate, batched on site to cut 4,000 truck movements from the town's roads.

Phase one topped out four weeks early in spring 2026; the promenade and the first residences open next summer. The joint venture's brief was a district that looks inevitable in twenty years, the kind of place visitors will assume was always there.

name: Kermia Hills Villas
summary: Eleven courtyard villas on a south-facing hillside above Nicosia, stone, shade and water arranged the way the island has always cooled its houses.

content.text: Kermia Hills places eleven villas on a terraced hillside at the capital's northern edge, each between 280 and 420 square metres, each organised around the oldest air-conditioning system on the island: a shaded courtyard with water at its centre. No two plans repeat, but every house follows the same section, thick walls to the west, deep verandas to the south, bedrooms opening east to the morning.

The hillside demanded respect before it gave views. Retaining structures were cast as habitable walls, wine stores, studies and garden rooms buried into the slope, rather than dead concrete, and every terrace's excavation was balanced on site so that not one truck of spoil left the hill. Ground-source loops beneath the driveways carry the heating and cooling load with photovoltaics on every roof.

Materials were chosen to age rather than to gleam: honed local stone, brushed lime plasters, thermally modified timber and brass ironmongery that will darken to the colour of the hillside's soil. Interiors were fitted by the group's own joinery workshop, the same shop that has built Characom stairs since 1993.

All eleven villas were occupied within a year of completion, and the planting along the shared lane has already knitted the terraces into the hill. The measure of a residential project is simple and slow: whether its owners' grandchildren will fight over it. Kermia Hills was built to make that argument worth having.

name: Ayia Napa Seafront Villas
summary: Six beachfront villas on the last undeveloped dunes east of Ayia Napa, glass pavilions held low behind replanted dune grass.

content.text: On a 300-metre strip of dune east of Ayia Napa, six villas sit in a single staggered line, none taller than the tamarisk trees that were there first. Each house is a pair of pavilions, a stone sleeping wing and a glass living wing, joined by a shaded breezeway that funnels the sea wind through the plan on the hottest days of August.

Building on sand is a foundation problem older than the town behind it. The villas bear on driven mini-piles socketed into the calcarenite bed nine metres down, with ground beams bridging the mobile dune layer entirely. The dune itself was rebuilt as part of the works, sand fencing and 14,000 planted grasses now hold a ridge that had been losing ground for years.

Seafront glazing at this exposure is specified like ship's equipment: cyclone-rated sliding walls, marine-anodised frames and glass that shrugs off salt spray without coatings that cloud. The stone wings are local calcarenite, quarried twenty kilometres away, laid by two master masons who signed their work inside a lintel.

Seven years on, the dune ridge stands a metre higher than at handover, and the villas have weathered from new to inevitable. It remains the project our residential clients most often ask to visit before commissioning their own.

name: Elaia Courtyard Homes
summary: Thirty-two family homes around three shared courtyards in Limassol's growing north, density done with gardens instead of corridors.

content.text: Elaia answers a question Limassol keeps asking as it grows: how to house families at real density without stacking them in corridors. Thirty-two two- and three-storey homes are arranged around three shared courtyards, car parking is pushed to the perimeter, and the space between front doors belongs to bicycles, fig trees and children, a village diagram executed with contemporary means.

The construction logic was repetition without monotony: a single structural cell, cross-wall concrete at a 5.4-metre rhythm, is rotated, mirrored and stepped to produce nine distinct house types. Repetition bought speed and precision; the stepping buys each home a roof terrace shaded by its neighbour's wall through the worst of the afternoon.

Finishes stay deliberately robust and repairable, self-coloured render, terrazzo stairs, hardwood screens, because a family home's finishes should survive a tricycle. Each courtyard drains to a buried cistern that irrigates the shared planting; the olive trees the project is named for were transplanted, already sixty years old, from the groves the development replaced.

A decade after completion, Elaia's resale values lead its district and the original owners' association still runs the harvest of its own olives every November. It was the group's 500th home milestone project, and remains its favourite argument that ordinary housing deserves extraordinary care.
